defines.meaning: source gives a definition or characterization of target

introduces.meaning: source introduces a mathematical item or proof component

assumes.meaning: source relies on target as an axiom, assumption, or theorem hypothesis

requires.meaning: source needs target to be stated, defined, constructed, or used

uses.meaning: source uses target as a result, method, tool, or construction

proves.meaning: source proof or proof step establishes target result

implies.meaning: source logically entails target in the stated context

equivalent_to.meaning: source and target are equivalent formulations in context

generalizes.meaning: source is a more general concept or result than target

specializes.meaning: source is a special case of target

subtype_of.meaning: source structure is a subtype of target structure

instance_of.meaning: source is an instance or model of target

inherits_from.meaning: source inherits structure or properties from target

constructed_from.meaning: source is constructed from target data

has_property.meaning: source satisfies or carries target property or structure

has_example.meaning: source has target as an example

has_counterexample.meaning: source is limited or clarified by target counterexample

shows_necessity_of.meaning: source demonstrates why target assumption or hypothesis is needed

violates_assumption.meaning: source fails target assumption, hypothesis, or property

motivates.meaning: source motivates target concept, theorem, construction, or question

prerequisite_for.meaning: source is a pedagogical prerequisite for target

applied_to.meaning: source result, method, or construction is applied to target

graph_semantics.node_rule: every node is a mathematical item
graph_semantics.view_rule: trees are extracted views, not the underlying graph

materialization_notes.outgoing_relations: Empty lists in item templates are a compact cache placeholder; materialize outgoing_relations from graph.edges by grouping on source when loading.

items.definition: The field studying spaces via open sets, continuity, convergence, compactness, connectedness, separation, and related invariants.
items.label: Topology

items.definition: A collection of elements treated as one object.
items.label: set

items.definition: A rule assigning each input exactly one output.
items.label: function

items.statement: A topology contains ∅ and X and is closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections.
items.label: topology axioms

items.statement: Every family of nonempty sets has a choice function.
items.label: axiom of choice

items.statement: The ambient space is Hausdorff.
items.label: Hausdorff assumption

items.statement: The ambient space is normal.
items.label: normality assumption

items.statement: The ambient space is regular.
items.label: regularity assumption

items.statement: The ambient space has a countable basis.
items.label: second-countability assumption

items.statement: The relevant space or family of spaces is compact.
items.label: compactness assumption

items.statement: The ambient space is locally compact.
items.label: local compactness assumption

items.statement: The metric space is complete.
items.label: complete metric assumption

items.definition: A pair (X, τ) of a set and a topology on it.
items.label: topological space

items.definition: A set with a pseudometric, allowing distinct points at distance zero.
items.label: pseudometric space

items.definition: A set with a distance function satisfying positivity, symmetry, and the triangle inequality.
items.intuition: Topology with numerical distance still visible.
items.label: metric space

items.definition: A subset equipped with the induced subspace topology.
items.label: subspace

items.definition: A Cartesian product equipped with the product topology.
items.label: product space

items.definition: A set of equivalence classes equipped with the quotient topology.
items.label: quotient space

items.definition: A topological space satisfying compactness.
items.label: compact space

items.definition: A topological space satisfying connectedness.
items.label: connected space

items.definition: A topological space satisfying path connectedness.
items.label: path-connected space

items.definition: A topological space satisfying the Hausdorff property.
items.label: Hausdorff space

items.definition: A topological space satisfying regularity.
items.label: regular space

items.definition: A topological space satisfying normality.
items.label: normal space

items.definition: A topological space satisfying local compactness.
items.label: locally compact space

items.definition: A topological space with a countable basis.
items.label: second-countable space

items.definition: A Hausdorff second-countable space locally homeomorphic to Euclidean space.
items.label: manifold

items.definition: A space that is both compact and Hausdorff.
items.label: compact Hausdorff space

items.statement: A topology on a set X is a collection of subsets of X containing ∅ and X and closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections.
items.formal_statement: \(\tau\subseteq \mathcal P(X),\ \varnothing,X\in\tau,\ \{U_i\}_{i\in I}\subseteq\tau\Rightarrow \bigcup_{i\in I}U_i\in\tau,\ \text{and } U_1,\ldots,U_n\in\tau\Rightarrow \bigcap_{k=1}^{n}U_k\in\tau.\)
items.definition: Given a set X, a topology τ on X is a specified collection of subsets of X, called open sets, satisfying the topology axioms: it contains ∅ and X, is closed under arbitrary unions, and is closed under finite intersections.
items.intuition: A topology records which subsets are open, thereby encoding local structure and continuity without choosing numerical distances.
equivalent_definitions: closed-set formulation via complements
items.label: topology

items.statement: In a topological space (X, τ), a subset U of X is open exactly when U belongs to τ.
items.formal_statement: \(U\subseteq X \text{ is open in }(X,\tau)\Longleftrightarrow U\in\tau.\)
items.definition: An open set of a topological space (X, τ) is a subset U⊆X that is an element of the topology τ.
items.intuition: Open sets are the primitive observable neighborhoods of the space; all topological notions are expressed in terms of them.
items.label: open set

items.statement: A subset C of a topological space X is closed exactly when its complement in X is open.
items.formal_statement: \(C\subseteq X \text{ is closed in }(X,\tau)\Longleftrightarrow X\setminus C\in\tau.\)
items.definition: For a topological space (X, τ), a subset C⊆X is closed in X if its complement X\C is open in X.
items.intuition: Closed sets are the subsets whose outside is topologically open; they contain their limit behavior as far as the topology detects it.
equivalent_definitions: member of the closed-set system dual to the topology
items.label: closed set

items.statement: A neighborhood of a point x is any subset containing an open set that contains x.
items.formal_statement: \(N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\Longleftrightarrow \exists U\in\tau\ \text{such that}\ x\in U\subseteq N.\)
items.definition: In a topological space (X, τ), a subset N⊆X is a neighborhood of x∈X if there is an open set U∈τ with x∈U⊆N. If N itself is open, it is an open neighborhood.
items.intuition: Neighborhoods are the sets large enough to contain some genuinely open region around the point.
equivalent_definitions: open neighborhood when the neighborhood itself is required to be open
items.label: neighborhood

items.statement: A basis for a topology is a collection of open sets such that every open set is a union of basis elements.
items.formal_statement: \(\mathcal B\subseteq\tau \text{ is a basis for }(X,\tau)\Longleftrightarrow \forall U\in\tau,\ U=\bigcup\{B\in\mathcal B: B\subseteq U\}.\)
items.definition: For a topological space (X, τ), a basis is a collection B of open subsets of X such that for every open set U and every point x∈U, there is B∈B with x∈B⊆U. Equivalently, every open set is a union of basis elements.
items.intuition: A basis gives small reusable open pieces from which all open sets are assembled by unions.
equivalent_definitions: covers X and locally refines finite intersections
items.label: basis for a topology

items.statement: A subbasis is a collection of subsets whose finite intersections form a basis for the topology it generates.
items.formal_statement: \(\langle\mathcal S\rangle=\left\{\bigcup_{\lambda\in\Lambda}\bigcap_{k=1}^{n_\lambda}S_{\lambda,k}: S_{\lambda,k}\in\mathcal S,\ n_\lambda<\infty\right\};\ \mathcal S \text{ is a subbasis for }\langle\mathcal S\rangle.\)
items.definition: For a set X, a subbasis S is a collection of subsets of X, usually with ⋃S=X, such that the finite intersections of members of S form a basis for the topology generated by S.
items.intuition: A subbasis specifies even simpler open constraints; finite intersections make basic opens, and arbitrary unions make all opens.
equivalent_definitions: coarsest topology containing the subbasis
items.label: subbasis

items.statement: The closure of A in X is the smallest closed subset of X containing A.
items.formal_statement: \(\operatorname{cl}_X(A)=\overline A=\bigcap\{F\subseteq X: A\subseteq F\ \text{and}\ F \text{ is closed in }X\}.\)
items.definition: For A⊆X, the closure cl_X(A) is the intersection of all closed subsets of X that contain A; equivalently, it is the least closed subset of X containing A.
items.intuition: The closure adds all points that cannot be separated from A by open neighborhoods.
equivalent_definitions: \(x\in\overline A\) iff every neighborhood of \(x\) intersects \(A\)
items.label: closure

items.statement: The interior of A in X is the largest open subset of X contained in A.
items.formal_statement: \(\operatorname{int}_X(A)=A^\circ=\bigcup\{U\in\tau: U\subseteq A\}.\)
items.definition: For A⊆X, the interior int_X(A) is the union of all open subsets of X contained in A; equivalently, it is the largest open subset of X contained in A.
items.intuition: The interior consists of points around which A contains an entire open neighborhood.
equivalent_definitions: \(x\in\operatorname{int}_X(A)\) iff \(A\) is a neighborhood of \(x\)
items.label: interior

items.statement: The boundary of A is the part of the closure of A outside the interior of A.
items.formal_statement: \(\partial_X A=\overline A\setminus A^\circ=\overline A\cap\overline{X\setminus A}.\)
items.definition: For A⊆X, the boundary ∂_X A is cl_X(A)\int_X(A). Equivalently, it is the set of points whose every neighborhood meets both A and X\A.
items.intuition: Boundary points are exactly the points where the topology cannot see a whole neighborhood lying entirely inside A or entirely outside A.
equivalent_definitions: \(\partial_X A=\overline A\cap\overline{X\setminus A}\)
every neighborhood meets both \(A\) and \(X\setminus A\)
items.label: boundary

items.statement: A subset A of X is dense in X exactly when its closure is all of X.
items.formal_statement: \(A\subseteq X \text{ is dense in }X\Longleftrightarrow \overline A=X\Longleftrightarrow \forall\,\varnothing\ne U\in\tau,\ U\cap A\ne\varnothing.\)
items.definition: For a topological space X, a subset A⊆X is dense in X if cl_X(A)=X. Equivalently, every nonempty open subset of X intersects A.
items.intuition: A dense subset is topologically unavoidable: every nonempty open region of the space contains a point of it.
equivalent_definitions: every nonempty open set intersects the subset
items.label: dense subset

items.statement: A map between topological spaces is continuous exactly when inverse images of open sets are open.
items.formal_statement: \(f:(X,\tau_X)\to(Y,\tau_Y)\text{ is continuous}\Longleftrightarrow \forall V\in\tau_Y,\ f^{-1}(V)\in\tau_X.\)
items.definition: Given topological spaces (X, τ_X) and (Y, τ_Y), a function f:X→Y is continuous if the preimage of every open subset of Y is open in X.
items.intuition: Continuity means that topological tests made by open sets in the codomain pull back to valid open tests in the domain.
equivalent_definitions: preimages of closed sets are closed
neighborhood formulation at each point
items.label: continuity

items.statement: A homeomorphism is a bijective continuous map whose inverse is also continuous.
items.formal_statement: \(f:X\to Y\text{ is a homeomorphism}\Longleftrightarrow f\text{ is bijective},\ f\text{ is continuous},\ \text{and }f^{-1}:Y\to X\text{ is continuous}.\)
items.definition: For topological spaces X and Y, a homeomorphism is a bijection f:X→Y such that both f and its inverse f^{-1}:Y→X are continuous. If such a map exists, X and Y are homeomorphic.
items.intuition: A homeomorphism is an isomorphism of topological spaces: it preserves and reflects exactly the open-set structure.
equivalent_definitions: bijective open continuous map
bijective closed continuous map
items.label: homeomorphism

items.statement: A net converges to x when it is eventually contained in every neighborhood of x.
items.formal_statement: \((x_i)_{i\in I}\to x\Longleftrightarrow \forall N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\ \exists i_0\in I\ \forall i\ge i_0,\ x_i\in N.\)
items.definition: In a topological space X, a net (x_i) indexed by a directed set I converges to x∈X if for every neighborhood N of x, the net is eventually in N. For sequences, I=ℕ with its usual order.
items.intuition: Convergence means that every topological test neighborhood of the proposed limit eventually contains all later terms.
equivalent_definitions: sequence version when the directed set is \(\mathbb N\)
items.label: convergence

items.statement: A topological space is compact when every open cover has a finite subcover.
items.formal_statement: \(X\text{ compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall\mathcal U\subseteq\tau,\ \left(X\subseteq\bigcup_{U\in\mathcal U}U\Rightarrow \exists U_1,\ldots,U_n\in\mathcal U\text{ with }X=\bigcup_{k=1}^{n}U_k\right).\)
items.definition: A topological space X is compact if every family of open subsets whose union contains X has a finite subfamily whose union still contains X. A subset A⊆X is compact when it is compact with the subspace topology.
items.intuition: Compactness says that any open-set description of the whole space already has a finite part that describes the whole space.
equivalent_definitions: compactness_fip_characterization
items.label: compactness

items.statement: A space is compact exactly when every closed family with the finite intersection property has nonempty total intersection.
items.formal_statement: \(X\text{ compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall\mathcal F\subseteq\{F\subseteq X:F\text{ closed}\},\ \left[\left(\forall F_1,\ldots,F_n\in\mathcal F,\ \bigcap_{k=1}^{n}F_k\ne\varnothing\right)\Rightarrow \bigcap_{F\in\mathcal F}F\ne\varnothing\right].\)
items.definition: The finite intersection property characterization says that compactness is equivalent to the following closed-set condition: whenever a family of closed subsets has nonempty intersection for every finite subfamily, the whole family has nonempty intersection.
items.intuition: This is the closed-set dual of the open-cover definition: failure of a finite subcover corresponds to finite intersections remaining nonempty.
items.notation: \(\mathcal F\) has the finite intersection property (FIP)
equivalent_definitions: open-cover definition of compactness
items.label: finite intersection property characterization

items.statement: A topological space is sequentially compact when every sequence has a convergent subsequence whose limit lies in the space.
items.formal_statement: \(X\text{ sequentially compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall (x_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\in X^{\mathbb N},\ \exists n_1<n_2<\cdots,\ \exists x\in X\text{ such that }x_{n_k}\to x.\)
items.definition: A topological space X is sequentially compact if every sequence in X has a subsequence that converges to a point of X.
items.intuition: Sequential compactness is a sequence-based finiteness property; in general topology it is not equivalent to open-cover compactness without additional hypotheses.
items.label: sequential compactness

items.statement: A topological space is connected when it cannot be separated into two disjoint nonempty open subsets.
items.formal_statement: \(X\text{ connected}\Longleftrightarrow \nexists U,V\in\tau\ \text{with }U,V\ne\varnothing,\ U\cap V=\varnothing,\ \text{and }X=U\cup V.\)
items.definition: A topological space X is connected if there do not exist disjoint nonempty open subsets U,V⊆X whose union is X. Equivalently, the only subsets of X that are both open and closed are ∅ and X.
items.intuition: Connectedness means the topology does not split the whole space into two separated open pieces.
equivalent_definitions: only clopen subsets are \(\varnothing\) and \(X\)
items.label: connectedness

items.statement: A topological space is path connected when every two points can be joined by a continuous path.
items.formal_statement: \(X\text{ path connected}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x,y\in X,\ \exists \gamma:[0,1]\to X\text{ continuous with }\gamma(0)=x,\ \gamma(1)=y.\)
items.definition: A topological space X is path connected if for every pair of points x,y∈X there is a continuous map γ:[0,1]→X from the unit interval to X with γ(0)=x and γ(1)=y.
items.intuition: Path connectedness is connectedness witnessed by actual continuous paths between points; it is stronger than connectedness in general.
equivalent_definitions: there is a single path component
items.label: path connectedness

items.statement: Separation axioms are topological conditions that distinguish points and closed sets by open sets or neighborhoods.
items.formal_statement:
\(T_0: orall x
e y\,\exists U\in	au\,[(x\in U\land y
otin U)\lor(y\in U\land x
otin U)],\quad T_2: orall x
e y\,\exists U,V\in	au\,(x\in U,\ y\in V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).\)
items.definition: Separation axioms are a family of properties, including T0, T1, Hausdorff/T2, regularity, and normality, that measure how well a topology separates points from points or points/closed sets from closed sets using open sets.
items.intuition: They quantify how much the topology can distinguish different points or closed regions.
items.label: separation axioms

items.statement: A topological space is Hausdorff if any two distinct points have disjoint open neighborhoods.
items.formal_statement: \((X,\tau)\text{ is Hausdorff}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x,y\in X,\ x\ne y\Rightarrow \exists U,V\in\tau\ (x\in U,\ y\in V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).\)
items.definition: A topological space X is Hausdorff, or T2, if for every pair of distinct points x,y∈X there are disjoint open sets U and V with x∈U and y∈V.
items.intuition: Hausdorffness says distinct points can be topologically pulled apart by nonoverlapping open regions.
equivalent_definitions: the diagonal \(\Delta_X\subseteq X\times X\) is closed, when product topology is available
items.label: Hausdorff property

items.statement: Under the common T1 convention, a space is regular if points and disjoint closed sets can be separated by disjoint open neighborhoods.
items.formal_statement: \((X,\tau)\text{ regular}\Longleftrightarrow [\forall x\in X,\ \{x\}\text{ is closed}]\ \land\ \forall x\in X\ \forall F\subseteq X,\ (F\text{ closed}\land x\notin F)\Rightarrow \exists U,V\in\tau\ (x\in U,\ F\subseteq V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).\)
items.definition: Using the common T1 convention, a topological space X is regular if singletons are closed and, whenever F is closed and x∉F, there are disjoint open sets U,V with x∈U and F⊆V.
items.intuition: Regularity separates a point from a closed obstacle by disjoint open regions.
equivalent_definitions: for every point x and neighborhood N of x, there is an open U with x∈U⊆cl(U)⊆N
items.label: regularity

items.statement: Under the common T1 convention, a space is normal if disjoint closed sets can be separated by disjoint open neighborhoods.
items.formal_statement: \((X,\tau)\text{ normal}\Longleftrightarrow [\forall x\in X,\ \{x\}\text{ is closed}]\ \land\ \forall F,G\subseteq X,\ (F,G\text{ closed}\land F\cap G=\varnothing)\Rightarrow \exists U,V\in\tau\ (F\subseteq U,\ G\subseteq V,\ U\cap V=\varnothing).\)
items.definition: Using the common T1 convention, a topological space X is normal if singletons are closed and every pair of disjoint closed subsets F,G⊆X has disjoint open neighborhoods U,V with F⊆U and G⊆V.
items.intuition: Normality is a strong separation property for closed sets, not just for individual points.
equivalent_definitions: Urysohn-function separation for normal spaces, in the setting of Urysohn's lemma
items.label: normality

items.statement: Countability axioms require bases or local neighborhood bases to be countable.
items.formal_statement: \(\text{First countable: }\forall x\in X\,\exists\mathcal B_x\subseteq\mathcal N_X(x)\ (|\mathcal B_x|\le\aleph_0\ \text{and }\mathcal B_x\text{ is local at }x);\quad \text{Second countable: }\exists\mathcal B\text{ basis for }\tau,\ |\mathcal B|\le\aleph_0.\)
items.definition: Countability axioms are topological size restrictions on local or global bases, most commonly first countability and second countability.
items.intuition: They say that the topology can be probed using only countably many local or global open pieces.
items.label: countability axioms

items.statement: A space is first countable if each point has a countable neighborhood basis.
items.formal_statement: \(X\text{ first countable}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x\in X\ \exists\mathcal B_x\subseteq\mathcal N_X(x)\ \left(|\mathcal B_x|\le\aleph_0\ \land\ \forall N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\ \exists B\in\mathcal B_x,\ x\in B\subseteq N\right).\)
items.definition: A topological space X is first countable if for every point x∈X there is a countable collection of neighborhoods of x such that every neighborhood of x contains one of them.
items.intuition: First countability gives each point a countable local scale of neighborhoods, making sequences more informative.
equivalent_definitions: each point has a countable local base
items.label: first countability

items.statement: A space is second countable if its topology has a countable basis.
items.formal_statement: \(X\text{ second countable}\Longleftrightarrow \exists\mathcal B\subseteq\tau\ \left(\mathcal B\text{ is a basis for }\tau\ \land\ |\mathcal B|\le\aleph_0\right).\)
items.definition: A topological space (X, τ) is second countable if there exists a countable basis for τ.
items.intuition: Second countability gives a countable global vocabulary for all open sets.
items.label: second countability

items.statement: A space is locally compact if every point has a compact neighborhood.
items.formal_statement: \(X\text{ locally compact}\Longleftrightarrow \forall x\in X\ \exists N\in\mathcal N_X(x)\ \text{such that }N\text{ is compact as a subspace of }X.\)
items.definition: A topological space X is locally compact if each point x∈X has a neighborhood N that is compact in the subspace topology. In Hausdorff settings this is often equivalently expressed using neighborhoods with compact closure.
items.intuition: Local compactness means that around every point there is a topologically finite region, even if the whole space is not compact.
equivalent_definitions: in Hausdorff spaces, each point has a neighborhood with compact closure
items.label: local compactness

items.definition: Open sets are A∩U where U is open in X.
items.label: subspace topology

items.definition: Topology generated by inverse images of opens under coordinate projections.
items.label: product topology

items.definition: The finest topology making the quotient map continuous.
items.label: quotient topology

items.definition: Topology generated by open metric balls.
items.label: metric topology

items.definition: Topology generated by intervals and rays in a linear order.
items.label: order topology

items.definition: Adjoin one point at infinity to a locally compact noncompact space.
items.label: one-point compactification

items.definition: Universal compact Hausdorff compactification for suitable spaces.
items.label: Stone-Cech compactification

items.definition: Coproduct topology making inclusions continuous.
items.label: disjoint union topology

items.definition: The finest topology making a family of maps into the space continuous.
items.label: final topology

items.definition: The coarsest topology making a family of maps out of the space continuous.
items.label: initial topology

items.definition: τ denotes a topology.
items.label: tau notation

items.definition: A family {U_i}_{i∈I} of open sets covering X.
items.label: open cover notation

items.definition: The product of indexed spaces is written ∏_{i∈I} X_i.
items.label: product notation

items.definition: Start with an arbitrary open cover and extract finite or local information.
items.label: open cover argument

items.definition: Show a cover has a finite subcover.
items.label: finite subcover argument

items.definition: Separate points or closed sets by open sets or functions.
items.label: separation argument

items.definition: Prove statements on a basis or subbasis.
items.label: basis argument

items.definition: Use the universal property of quotient topology.
items.label: quotient argument

items.definition: Use compactness to replace infinite data by finite data.
items.label: compactness argument

items.definition: Use the nonexistence of separations or connected images.
items.label: connectedness argument

items.definition: Extract coherent subsequences or choices by diagonalization.
items.label: diagonal argument

items.definition: Generalized convergence tools beyond sequences.
items.label: nets and filters

items.definition: Patch local data using subordinate partitions of unity.
items.label: partition of unity argument

items.statement: If every chain has an upper bound, then a maximal element exists.
items.label: Zorn's lemma

items.statement: In a Hausdorff space, compact subsets are closed.
items.label: compact subsets of Hausdorff spaces are closed

items.statement: A finite product of compact spaces is compact.
items.label: finite product compactness corollary

items.statement: A closed simply connected 3-manifold is homeomorphic to the 3-sphere.
items.label: Poincare conjecture

items.statement: Use topology to formulate convergence, continuity, compactness, and fixed points in metric settings.
items.label: analysis on metric spaces

items.statement: Applications of fixed point theorems to analysis and dynamics.
items.label: fixed point applications

items.statement: Use circles, tori, and Hawaiian earrings as testing spaces.
items.label: algebraic topology examples

items.conclusion: K is compact iff K is closed and bounded.

variants.label: Heine-Borel on R
variants.conclusion: K is compact iff K is closed and bounded.

items.label: Heine-Borel theorem

items.conclusion: The sequence has a convergent subsequence.
items.label: Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem

hypotheses: A and B disjoint closed subsets
items.conclusion: There is continuous f:X→[0,1] with f(A)=0 and f(B)=1.
items.label: Urysohn lemma

items.conclusion: f extends to a continuous F:X→[a,b].
items.label: Tietze extension theorem

hypotheses: S is a subbasis for X
items.conclusion: Compactness can be tested on subbasic open covers.
items.label: Alexander subbase theorem

hypotheses: {X_i}_{i∈I} is a family of compact spaces
items.label: Tychonoff theorem

hypotheses: X complete metric or locally compact Hausdorff
items.conclusion: Countable intersections of dense open sets are dense.
items.label: Baire category theorem

hypotheses: C is a simple closed curve in the plane
items.conclusion: R^2\C has exactly two connected components with common boundary C.
items.label: Jordan curve theorem

items.strategy: Use product subbases and Alexander subbase theorem; choice appears through maximal or coordinatewise selection.
items.label: Proof of Tychonoff via Alexander subbase theorem

items.claim: It suffices to test compactness on subbasic covers.
items.justification: Apply Alexander subbase theorem to the product subbasis.
items.label: Tychonoff step: reduce to subbasic cover

items.claim: Coherent choices produce a point avoiding a cover when no finite subcover exists.
items.justification: Use choice or an equivalent maximality argument.
items.label: Tychonoff step: choose coordinates

items.claim: Compactness of factors contradicts failure of finite subcover.
items.justification: Project subbasic data to factors and extract finite subcovers.
items.label: Tychonoff step: finite subcover contradiction

items.strategy: Reduce closed bounded subsets of R^n to compact cubes and finite subcovers.
items.label: Proof of Heine-Borel by open covers

items.claim: A bounded Euclidean set lies in a compact cube.
items.justification: Use interval compactness and finite subcovers.
items.label: Heine-Borel step: compact cube

items.claim: Closed subsets of compact Euclidean spaces are compact.
items.justification: Convert covers by using the open complement of a closed set.
items.label: Heine-Borel step: closed subset

items.definition: R with its usual metric topology.
items.label: real line with standard topology

items.definition: R^n with its standard metric topology.
items.label: Euclidean space

items.definition: A space in which every subset is open.
items.label: discrete space

items.definition: A space with only ∅ and X open.
items.label: indiscrete space

items.definition: The two-point space with open sets ∅, {1}, and {0,1}.
items.label: Sierpinski space

items.definition: The middle-thirds Cantor subset of [0,1].
items.label: Cantor set

items.definition: S^1 with the subspace topology from R^2.
items.label: circle

items.definition: S^1×S^1, equivalently a square with opposite sides identified.
items.label: torus

items.definition: Q with the subspace topology inherited from R.
items.label: rationals with subspace topology

items.definition: A non-second-countable ordered topological space built from uncountable ordinals.
items.label: long line

items.definition: R with the lower-limit topology generated by [a,b).
items.label: Sorgenfrey line

items.definition: A union of countably many tangent circles with radii tending to zero.
items.label: Hawaiian earring

items.definition: The closure of the graph of sin(1/x) for x>0.
items.label: topologist's sine curve

items.definition: Spaces formed by identifying points, such as endpoints of an interval.
items.label: quotient space examples

items.definition: A set considered without a specified topology.
items.label: bare set without topology

items.definition: Sierpinski or indiscrete-type spaces showing failures after removing Hausdorff hypotheses.
items.label: non-Hausdorff spaces as counterexamples

items.definition: A compact space in which sequences do not detect compactness, such as certain large products.
items.label: compact non-sequentially compact space

items.definition: A connected space such as the topologist's sine curve that is not path connected.
items.label: connected not path-connected space

edges.label: A topology defines the structure on a topological space
A topological space is defined by a topology on a set
Topology definition uses the topology axioms
edges.notes: The axioms specify the closure conditions on the collection of open sets.

edges.label: Basis elements are open in the topology they generate or describe
edges.notes: For a basis for a specified topology, basis elements are open and every open set is their union.

edges.label: Closure is defined by closed sets
Interior is defined by open sets
Continuity is a property of functions
Continuity uses preimages of open sets
Open-cover compactness is equivalent to FIP compactness
Connectedness is defined by absence of open separations
Hausdorff separation uses disjoint open neighborhoods
Regularity separates points from closed sets
edges.notes: The regularity definition uses the common T1 convention.

edges.label: Normality separates disjoint closed sets
edges.notes: The normality definition uses the common T1 convention.

edges.label: First countability is a countability axiom
Second countability is a countability axiom
Local compactness is local around neighborhoods
Metric spaces are pseudometric spaces with separation
Regular Hausdorff spaces specialize Hausdorff spaces
edges.notes: Some conventions define regular without T1; this graph uses the common regular Hausdorff convention.

edges.label: Normal Hausdorff spaces specialize regular Hausdorff spaces
edges.notes: Convention-dependent; here normal includes the relevant separation assumptions.

edges.label: Locally compact spaces are topological spaces
Path connectedness implies connectedness
edges.notes: Path connectedness is not needed to define connectedness; it is a stronger property.

edges.label: Normal T1 spaces are regular
edges.notes: Valid under the T1-included convention used in the normality definition.

edges.label: Regular T1 spaces are Hausdorff
edges.notes: Valid under the T1-included convention used in the regularity definition.

edges.label: Local compactness defines locally compact spaces
Subspace topology is built from an ambient topological space
Subspace opens are intersections with ambient opens
Product topology is built from factor spaces
Product topology is generated by a subbasis
Quotient topology is built from a topological domain
Quotient topology is defined by continuity of the quotient map
Metric topology is generated from a metric
Metric topology induces a topological space
Order topology is generated by interval bases
One-point compactification requires local compactness for Hausdorff behavior
Stone-Cech needs strong regularity conditions
edges.notes: A full treatment uses complete regularity/Tychonoff spaces.

edges.label: Disjoint union topology is built from a family of spaces
Final topology is built from maps into a set
Final topology is defined by continuity
Initial topology is built from maps out of a set
Initial topology is defined by continuity
Open-cover notation is central to compactness
Product notation is used with product topology
Finite product compactness follows from Tychonoff
Heine-Borel is stated in Euclidean space
Urysohn lemma requires normality in its statement
Urysohn lemma is about closed sets
Urysohn lemma constructs a continuous function
Normality is an assumption of Urysohn lemma
Tietze theorem requires normality to state
Tietze theorem is about closed subsets
Tietze extension theorem uses Urysohn lemma
Normality is an assumption of Tietze theorem
Alexander theorem is stated for subbases
A standard proof uses Zorn's lemma
Compactness is formulated through open covers
A standard proof uses Alexander subbase theorem
Choice-equivalent maximality can appear in proofs
Arbitrary Tychonoff uses choice in standard set theory
Baire theorem has a complete metric variant
Baire theorem has a locally compact variant
Locally compact version uses Hausdorff separation
Baire theorem is about dense open sets
Complete metric assumption for one Baire variant
Local compactness assumption for one Baire variant
Hausdorff assumption for locally compact Baire variant
The proof uses Urysohn-type separation functions
Brouwer theorem is about continuous maps
Compactness of disks follows from Heine-Borel
Jordan theorem is about simple closed curves
Jordan theorem is about the plane
Cantor set is an example of compactness
Indiscrete spaces need not be Hausdorff
They show why Hausdorff hypotheses matter
Non-Hausdorff counterexamples clarify the Hausdorff hypothesis
edges.notes: Changed from has_counterexample because proposition nodes are not valid sources for has_counterexample in the schema.

edges.label: Compactness need not be sequential compactness
Sequential criteria often need countability assumptions
A topology is defined on an underlying set
Open sets are members of a topology
Separation axioms are formulated using open sets
Regular and normal separation axioms use closed sets

concept_map.description: Concept-level map of spaces, definitions, examples, and motivating relations.
concept_map.example_traversal: topological_space -> defined_by -> topology; compactness -> has_example -> cantor_set; quotient_space_examples -> motivates -> quotient_topology

definition_dependency_graph.description: Definitions needed to state concepts, structures, and theorems.
definition_dependency_graph.example_traversal: tychonoff_theorem -> requires -> product_space, product_topology, compactness, open_set

theorem_dependency_graph.description: Logical dependencies among results and assumptions, separated from definitional needs.
theorem_dependency_graph.example_traversal: tychonoff_theorem -> uses -> alexander_subbase_theorem -> uses -> zorns_lemma; tychonoff_theorem -> assumes -> axiom_of_choice

proof_graph.description: Proofs, proof steps, proof methods, and results used by particular proofs.

inheritance_tree.description: Tree-like or DAG-like inheritance view of mathematical structures; multiple inheritance is retained.
inheritance_tree.example_traversal: incoming subtype_of from topological_space, then from compact_space and hausdorff_space to compact_hausdorff_space

construction_graph.description: How spaces and topologies are constructed from prior data.
construction_graph.example_traversal: product_topology -> constructed_from -> subbasis; product_topology -> induces -> product_space

prerequisite_map.description: Pedagogical order; useful for learning but not always logical necessity.
prerequisite_map.example_traversal: set -> topology -> open_set -> compactness -> alexander_subbase_theorem -> tychonoff_theorem

application_map.description: Where theorems, methods, and constructions are applied or illustrated.
application_map.example_traversal: brouwer_fixed_point_theorem -> applied_to -> fixed_point_applications; jordan_curve_theorem -> has_example -> circle

traversal_primitives.outgoing: Follow edges whose source is the current node.
traversal_primitives.incoming: Follow edges whose target is the current node.
traversal_primitives.both: Follow incoming and outgoing edges while preserving direction in returned paths.
traversal_primitives.multigraph_rule: Do not collapse parallel edges; edge type and dependency_class determine meaning.

dependency_semantics.definitions_needed_to_state: requires edges with dependency_class definitional_dependency or notation_dependency
dependency_semantics.proof_tools_used: uses edges from theorem, proof, or proof_step to proof_method, lemma, theorem, proposition, or axiom

example_queries.example: From tychonoff_theorem return product_space, product_topology, compactness, open_set, product_notation, open_cover_notation, alexander_subbase_theorem, zorns_lemma, and axiom_of_choice grouped by dependency_class.
From urysohn_metrization_theorem follow requires to regularity, hausdorff_property, second_countability, and metric_topology.
From heine_borel_theorem keep targets of kind proof_method: open_cover_argument, finite_subcover_argument, compactness_argument.
From tychonoff_theorem follow assumes to axiom_of_choice; from urysohn_metrization_theorem follow assumes to regularity_assumption, hausdorff_assumption, and second_countability_assumption.
From compactness follow has_example to cantor_set and circle; also inspect incoming has_property from examples for additional satisfying instances.
From hausdorff_assumption use incoming shows_necessity_of to get non_hausdorff_spaces_as_counterexamples, then inspect compact_subsets_of_hausdorff_are_closed -> has_counterexample.
From topological_space follow incoming subtype_of; compact_hausdorff_space appears under both compact_space and hausdorff_space.

One path is set -> topology -> open_set -> compactness -> alexander_subbase_theorem -> tychonoff_theorem via prerequisite_for; enrich with tychonoff_theorem -> requires -> product_topology and tychonoff_theorem -> assumes -> axiom_of_choice.
